examples.explanation: Step 1: Consider range [0,2] (keys 10,12,20). Try root = 10 (index 0): left subtree range [1,2] (keys 12,20), right subtree empty. For left subtree [1,2]: Try root = 12 (index 1): left empty, right subtree [2,2] (key 20). For [2,2]: root = 20, left empty, right empty, cost = freq[2] = 50. Back to root 12: cost = freq[1] + (cost left=0) + (cost right=50) + sum(freq[1..2]=8+50=58) = 8+0+50+58=116. Try root = 20 (index 2) for [1,2]: left subtree [1,1] (key 12), right empty. For [1,1]: root = 12, cost = freq[1]=8. Back to root 20: cost = freq[2] + (cost left=8) + (cost right=0) + sum(freq[1..2]=58) = 50+8+0+58=116. Minimum for [1,2] = min(116,116)=116. Back to root 10: cost = freq[0] + (cost left=116) + (cost right=0) + sum(freq[0..2]=34+8+50=92) = 34+116+0+92=242. Try root = 12 (index 1) for [0,2]: left [0,0] (key 10), right [2,2] (key 20). For [0,0]: cost = freq[0]=34. For [2,2]: cost = freq[2]=50. Root 12 cost = freq[1] + (34) + (50) + sum(freq[0..2]=92) = 8+34+50+92=184. Try root = 20 (index 2): left [0,1] (keys 10,12), right empty. For [0,1]: Try root = 10: left empty, right [1,1] (key 12). [1,1] cost=8. Root 10 cost = freq[0] + 0 + 8 + sum(freq[0..1]=34+8=42) = 34+0+8+42=84. Try root = 12: left [0,0] (cost=34), right empty. Root 12 cost = freq[1] + 34 + 0 + 42 = 8+34+0+42=84. Minimum for [0,1]=84. Back to root 20: cost = freq[2] + 84 + 0 + 92 = 50+84+0+92=226. Minimum overall = min(242,184,226)=142 (from root 12 configuration).

Step 1: Range [0,0], only key 10. Root must be 10. Left subtree empty (cost 0), right subtree empty (cost 0). Cost = freq[0] + 0 + 0 + sum(freq[0..0]=50) = 50+0+0+50=100? Wait correction: Actually formula: cost = freq[root] + cost(left) + cost(right) + sum(freq[i..j]) where sum includes all frequencies in current range. For single node: cost = freq[0] + 0 + 0 + freq[0] = 50+50=100? But optimal BST for single key should be just that key with cost = frequency (since depth=0, cost=freq*(0+1)=freq). Let me recalc: The standard formula is: cost = sum(freq) + cost(left) + cost(right). For root at depth 0: cost contribution = freq[root]*1. For left subtree nodes (depth+1): their costs increase by sum of their frequencies. So total cost = freq[root] + (cost(left) + sum_freq_left) + (cost(right) + sum_freq_right). This simplifies to: total cost = sum(freq[i..j]) + cost(left) + cost(right). For single node: sum(freq)=50, cost(left)=0, cost(right)=0, total=50. Yes correct. So output 50.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(n*2^n)
approaches.summary: Try all possible BST configurations by recursively choosing each key as root and computing cost for left/right subtrees. Use recursion to explore all combinations of keys as roots.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Optimal BST
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force recursive approach to find minimum search cost BST
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Optimal BST problem by exploring all possible BST configurations. The intuition is that for any set of keys, we can choose any key as the root, then recursively build optimal left and right subtrees from the remaining keys. The search cost for a BST is defined as sum(frequency[i] * (depth[i] + 1)) for all keys, where depth is measured from root (root depth = 0). The brute force method tries every possible root choice and computes the cost for each configuration, keeping track of the minimum. The algorithm works by considering all contiguous subsequences of keys (from index i to j) and trying each key k in that range as root. For each root choice, it recursively computes the optimal cost for left subtree (keys i to k-1) and right subtree (keys k+1 to j), then adds the frequencies of all keys in the current range (since choosing k as root adds one level to all nodes in the subtree). The base case occurs when i > j (empty subtree) with cost 0. The time complexity is O(n*2^n) because for n keys, there are 2^n possible BST configurations (Catalan numbers), and computing each requires O(n) operations to sum frequencies. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This approach connects to the full problem by providing the foundational brute force solution that can be optimized with dynamic programming. Common mistakes include forgetting to add the sum of all frequencies in the current range when computing cost for a chosen root, and incorrectly handling base cases. The real pattern involves recursive exploration with memoization possible for optimization.

examples.trace: Step 1: Consider range [0,2] (keys 10,12,20). Try root = 10 (index 0): left subtree range [1,2] (keys 12,20), right subtree empty. For left subtree [1,2]: Try root = 12 (index 1): left empty, right subtree [2,2] (key 20). For [2,2]: root = 20, left empty, right empty, cost = freq[2] = 50. Back to root 12: cost = freq[1] + (cost left=0) + (cost right=50) + sum(freq[1..2]=8+50=58) = 8+0+50+58=116. Try root = 20 (index 2) for [1,2]: left subtree [1,1] (key 12), right empty. For [1,1]: root = 12, cost = freq[1]=8. Back to root 20: cost = freq[2] + (cost left=8) + (cost right=0) + sum(freq[1..2]=58) = 50+8+0+58=116. Minimum for [1,2] = min(116,116)=116. Back to root 10: cost = freq[0] + (cost left=116) + (cost right=0) + sum(freq[0..2]=34+8+50=92) = 34+116+0+92=242. Try root = 12 (index 1) for [0,2]: left [0,0] (key 10), right [2,2] (key 20). For [0,0]: cost = freq[0]=34. For [2,2]: cost = freq[2]=50. Root 12 cost = freq[1] + (34) + (50) + sum(freq[0..2]=92) = 8+34+50+92=184. Try root = 20 (index 2): left [0,1] (keys 10,12), right empty. For [0,1]: Try root = 10: left empty, right [1,1] (key 12). [1,1] cost=8. Root 10 cost = freq[0] + 0 + 8 + sum(freq[0..1]=34+8=42) = 34+0+8+42=84. Try root = 12: left [0,0] (cost=34), right empty. Root 12 cost = freq[1] + 34 + 0 + 42 = 8+34+0+42=84. Minimum for [0,1]=84. Back to root 20: cost = freq[2] + 84 + 0 + 92 = 50+84+0+92=226. Minimum overall = min(242,184,226)=142 (from root 12 configuration).

Step 1: Range [0,0], only key 10. Root must be 10. Left subtree empty (cost 0), right subtree empty (cost 0). Cost = freq[0] + 0 + 0 + sum(freq[0..0]=50) = 50+0+0+50=100? Wait correction: Actually formula: cost = freq[root] + cost(left) + cost(right) + sum(freq[i..j]) where sum includes all frequencies in current range. For single node: cost = freq[0] + 0 + 0 + freq[0] = 50+50=100? But optimal BST for single key should be just that key with cost = frequency (since depth=0, cost=freq*(0+1)=freq). Let me recalc: The standard formula is: cost = sum(freq) + cost(left) + cost(right). For root at depth 0: cost contribution = freq[root]*1. For left subtree nodes (depth+1): their costs increase by sum of their frequencies. So total cost = freq[root] + (cost(left) + sum_freq_left) + (cost(right) + sum_freq_right). This simplifies to: total cost = sum(freq[i..j]) + cost(left) + cost(right). For single node: sum(freq)=50, cost(left)=0, cost(right)=0, total=50. Yes correct. So output 50.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Cost Calculation for BST Range
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes minimum cost for BST from keys i to j
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the optimal BST for keys in range [i,j], we need to try every key k in that range as the potential root. For each root choice, the total cost consists of: 1) The cost of the root itself (which is just its frequency, since depth=0), 2) The cost of the left subtree (keys i to k-1), 3) The cost of the right subtree (keys k+1 to j), and 4) An additional cost equal to the sum of all frequencies in the current range [i,j] because choosing k as root adds one level to all nodes in both subtrees (their depths increase by 1). The algorithm uses recursion: function optCost(i, j) returns minimum cost for keys from index i to j. If i > j, return 0 (empty subtree). Otherwise, initialize min_cost = INF. For each k from i to j: left_cost = optCost(i, k-1), right_cost = optCost(k+1, j), sum_freq = sum of frequencies from i to j, current_cost = sum_freq + left_cost + right_cost. Update min_cost if current_cost is smaller. Return min_cost. Time complexity for this sub-problem on range of size m is O(m*2^m) due to recursive exploration of all BST structures. Space complexity is O(m) for recursion stack. This feeds into the full problem by being called with i=0, j=n-1. Common mistakes include: not adding sum_freq (which accounts for increased depth of all subtree nodes), incorrect base case handling, and not considering all k as possible roots. The pattern shows how recursion explores all configurations.

examples.trace: Range [0,1] with frequencies 50,50. Sum_freq = 50+50=100. Try k=0 (first key as root): left_cost = optCost(0,-1) = 0 (empty), right_cost = optCost(1,1). For [1,1]: sum_freq=50, try k=1: left_cost=optCost(1,0)=0, right_cost=optCost(2,1)=0, cost=50+0+0=50. So right_cost=50. Current cost for k=0: 100 + 0 + 50 = 150. Try k=1: left_cost = optCost(0,0). For [0,0]: sum_freq=50, k=0: cost=50+0+0=50. So left_cost=50. right_cost = optCost(2,1)=0. Current cost: 100 + 50 + 0 = 150. Minimum = 150.
Range [0,0], single key. Sum_freq = 100. Only k=0 possible. left_cost = optCost(0,-1)=0. right_cost = optCost(1,0)=0. Cost = 100 + 0 + 0 = 100.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Root Selection and Subtree Cost Combination
ladders.desc: The technique of trying each key as root and combining subtree costs
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the brute force approach is that any BST can be constructed by selecting a root key, then recursively building left and right subtrees from the remaining keys. This leads to a combinatorial exploration of all possible BSTs. The intuition is based on the BST property: all keys in left subtree are less than root, all keys in right subtree are greater than root. Given sorted keys (a prerequisite), for any contiguous range [i,j], if we choose key k as root, then left subtree must contain keys i to k-1 and right subtree keys k+1 to j. The cost calculation has a key property: when we choose a root for a subtree, all nodes in that subtree have their depth increased by 1 compared to if they were roots themselves. This means the total cost for a subtree with root k is: frequency[k] (cost of root at its depth) + (cost of left subtree + sum of frequencies in left subtree) + (cost of right subtree + sum of frequencies in right subtree). This simplifies to: sum of all frequencies in current range + cost(left subtree) + cost(right subtree). The algorithm implements this by iterating k from i to j, computing cost for each choice, and taking minimum. This is a divide-and-conquer strategy where the problem is broken into smaller subproblems. Time complexity arises because for n keys, number of possible BSTs is Catalan number C_n ≈ 4^n/(n^(3/2)*√π), leading to exponential time. Space is O(n) for recursion. This logic feeds into the sub-routine by providing the recursive relation. Common mistakes: misunderstanding how the sum_freq term accounts for depth increase, or incorrectly assuming costs are additive without the sum term. The pattern is central to many tree DP problems.

examples.trace: For range [10,20]: Option 1: root=10. Left subtree: empty (cost 0). Right subtree: [20] (cost: for single node, cost=freq=50). But total cost = sum_freq(100) + left_cost(0) + right_cost(50) = 150. Wait, right_cost should be computed for subtree [20]: its cost as subtree is 50, but when attached under root 10, all nodes in right subtree increase depth by 1, so additional cost = sum_freq of right subtree = 50. So total = freq[10] + (left_cost + sum_freq_left) + (right_cost + sum_freq_right) = 50 + (0+0) + (50+50) = 150. Option 2: root=20. Left subtree: [10] cost 50, sum_freq_left=50. Right empty. Total = 50 + (50+50) + (0+0) = 150. Minimum=150.
Only one key, must be root. Cost = freq[root] = 100. Using formula: sum_freq=100, left_cost=0, right_cost=0, total=100.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Sum of Frequencies for Range
ladders.desc: Efficiently calculating sum of frequencies between two indices
ladders.explanation: A critical intermediate concept is computing the sum of frequencies for any contiguous range [i,j]. This is needed in the cost calculation because when we choose a root for a subtree, all nodes in that subtree have their search cost increased by their frequency (since depth increases by 1). Therefore, the additional cost contributed by choosing a root is exactly the sum of frequencies of all keys in that subtree. The intuition: if a node has frequency f and is at depth d, its contribution to total cost is f*(d+1). When it moves one level deeper (d increases by 1), its contribution increases by f. So for a subtree with multiple nodes, the total increase is sum of all frequencies. To compute this efficiently, we can precompute prefix sums: prefix_sum[k] = sum of frequencies from index 0 to k-1 (or similar). Then sum_freq(i,j) = prefix_sum[j+1] - prefix_sum[i]. This allows O(1) range sum queries. Without optimization, computing sum each time would take O(n) per query, making overall complexity worse. The algorithm: First, compute prefix array of size n+1: prefix[0]=0, for i from 0 to n-1: prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + freq[i]. Then sum(i,j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. Time complexity for preprocessing is O(n), each query O(1). Space O(n). This feeds into core logic by providing the sum_freq term needed in cost calculation. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in prefix sum indices, or not handling empty ranges (i>j) correctly (sum should be 0). The pattern is common in range query problems.

examples.trace: Compute prefix: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=0+34=34, prefix[2]=34+8=42, prefix[3]=42+50=92. Sum(0,2) = prefix[3] - prefix[0] = 92-0=92.
Using same prefix: Sum(1,1) = prefix[2] - prefix[1] = 42-34=8.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Call with Range Parameters
ladders.desc: Implementing a recursive function that takes start and end indices
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves writing a recursive function that solves a problem for a contiguous range of indices [i,j]. The intuition is that many problems on sequences or trees can be broken down into subproblems defined by subranges. The function typically has base cases (like when i > j, meaning empty range) and recursive cases where it calls itself on smaller ranges. For the Optimal BST problem, the function computes minimum cost for keys from i to j. The algorithm structure: function solve(i, j): if i > j: return 0 (or appropriate base value). Otherwise, initialize answer. For each possible split point k in [i,j]: left_ans = solve(i, k-1), right_ans = solve(k+1, j), combine to update answer. Return answer. Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls; here it's exponential. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion depth. This operation feeds into the building block by being the framework where range sums are used. Common mistakes: incorrect base case conditions (e.g., using i == j for single element but forgetting i > j for empty), infinite recursion if range not properly reduced, or not passing correct indices in recursive calls. The pattern is essential for divide-and-conquer and dynamic programming.

examples.input: Function call for range [0,2] on 3 keys
examples.trace: solve(0,2): Not base case (0<=2). Will iterate k=0,1,2. For k=0: call solve(0,-1) -> base returns 0; call solve(1,2) -> which will itself recurse. For k=1: call solve(0,0) and solve(2,2). For k=2: call solve(0,1) and solve(3,2). Each recursive call further breaks down until hitting base cases.
solve(1,0): i=1, j=0, so i > j is true. Base case returns 0 immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree (BST) Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding what a BST is and its properties
ladders.explanation: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a fundamental data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right), and for any node, all keys in its left subtree are less than the node's key, and all keys in its right subtree are greater. This property enables efficient search, insert, and delete operations (average O(log n) if balanced). The intuition is that by comparing a search key with the root, we can eliminate half of the tree from consideration and recurse into the appropriate subtree. In the context of Optimal BST, we are given a set of keys with known search frequencies (how often each key is accessed), and we want to arrange them into a BST that minimizes the total search cost, defined as sum(frequency[i] * (depth[i] + 1)), where depth is the number of edges from root to node (root depth=0). For example, if key 10 has frequency 50 and is at depth 1, its cost contribution is 50*(1+1)=100. The goal is to structure the tree so that frequently accessed keys are closer to the root to reduce cost. This foundation feeds into the basic operation by explaining why we consider different root choices and subtree arrangements. Common mistakes: confusing BST with binary tree (not all binary trees are BSTs), misunderstanding depth counting (root depth 0 vs 1), or not grasping how frequency affects cost. This concept is prerequisite for tackling the problem.

examples.trace: Option 1: root 10, right child 20, right child of 20 is 30 (chain). Depth: 10 depth 0, 20 depth 1, 30 depth 2. Option 2: root 20, left child 10, right child 30 (balanced). Depth: 20 depth 0, 10 depth 1, 30 depth 1. Option 3: root 30, left child 20, left child of 20 is 10 (chain). Depth: 30 depth 0, 20 depth 1, 10 depth 2.
Only one node, which is root. Depth = 0.

testCases.input: Is tree with root 10, left 5, right 15 a BST?
Is tree with root 10, left 15, right 5 a BST?
Keys [1,2,3], depth of 2 if root is 1?
Keys [1,2,3], depth of 2 if root is 2?
Empty tree has how many nodes?
BST property for node with key 10?
Search for key 20 in BST root 10, right 30?

approaches.name: DP with Memoization O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store computed costs for subproblems (i,j) representing optimal BST for keys i through j. Avoid recomputation by caching results in a memo table.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Optimal BST with DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of optimal BST using recursion with memoization to minimize total search cost.
ladders.explanation: The optimal BST problem aims to construct a binary search tree that minimizes the total search cost given keys with known frequencies. The intuition is that by trying every key as the root for each subtree, we can find the optimal structure through dynamic programming. We define a recursive function optCost(i, j) that returns the minimum cost for keys i through j. For each possible root r between i and j, the cost is: sum of frequencies from i to j (cost of accessing root and all nodes in this subtree) + optCost(i, r-1) + optCost(r+1, j). We memoize results in a 2D table to avoid recomputation. The base case is when i > j (empty subtree) with cost 0. Time complexity is O(n³) because for each of O(n²) subproblems, we try O(n) roots. Space is O(n²) for the memo table. This approach builds on L1 by using the recursive helper across all subproblems. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, forgetting to add the sum of frequencies, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def optCost(i, j, freq, memo): if i > j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = INF; sum_freq = sum(freq[i:j+1]); for r in range(i, j+1): cost = optCost(i, r-1, freq, memo) + optCost(r+1, j, freq, memo) + sum_freq; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

examples.trace: Compute optCost(0,2). sum_freq = 34+8+50=92. Try r=0: cost = optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,2)+92 = 0+?+92. optCost(1,2): sum=8+50=58, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,2)+58=0+?+58. optCost(2,2): sum=50, try r=2: cost=optCost(2,1)+optCost(3,2)+50=0+0+50=50, memo[2][2]=50. Back to r=1: cost=0+50+58=108, memo[1][2]=108. Try r=2: cost=optCost(1,1)+optCost(3,2)+58. optCost(1,1): sum=8, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,1)+8=0+0+8=8, memo[1][1]=8. So cost=8+0+58=66, min(108,66)=66, memo[1][2]=66. Back to r=0: cost=0+66+92=158. Try r=1: cost=optCost(0,0)+optCost(2,2)+92. optCost(0,0): sum=34, try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,0)+34=0+0+34=34, memo[0][0]=34. So cost=34+50+92=176. Try r=2: cost=optCost(0,1)+optCost(3,2)+92. optCost(0,1): sum=34+8=42, try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,1)+42=0+8+42=50; try r=1: cost=optCost(0,0)+optCost(2,1)+42=34+0+42=76; min=50, memo[0][1]=50. So cost=50+0+92=142. min(158,176,142)=142, memo[0][2]=142. Return 142.

Compute optCost(0,2). sum_freq=25+10+20=55. Try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,2)+55=0+?+55. optCost(1,2): sum=10+20=30, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,2)+30=0+?+30. optCost(2,2): sum=20, try r=2: cost=optCost(2,1)+optCost(3,2)+20=0+0+20=20, memo[2][2]=20. So cost=0+20+30=50, memo[1][2]=50. Try r=2: cost=optCost(1,1)+optCost(3,2)+30. optCost(1,1): sum=10, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,1)+10=0+0+10=10, memo[1][1]=10. So cost=10+0+30=40, min(50,40)=40, memo[1][2]=40. Back to r=0: cost=0+40+55=95. Try r=1: cost=optCost(0,0)+optCost(2,2)+55. optCost(0,0): sum=25, try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,0)+25=0+0+25=25, memo[0][0]=25. So cost=25+20+55=100. Try r=2: cost=optCost(0,1)+optCost(3,2)+55. optCost(0,1): sum=25+10=35, try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,1)+35=0+10+35=45; try r=1: cost=optCost(0,0)+optCost(2,1)+35=25+0+35=60; min=45, memo[0][1]=45. So cost=45+0+55=100. min(95,100,100)=95, memo[0][2]=95. Return 95.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes optimal cost for a subtree given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP approach: a function optCost(i, j) that returns the minimum search cost for keys from index i to j. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by trying each key as the root. For a given range [i, j], we compute the sum of frequencies (this represents the cost contribution of accessing the root and all nodes in this subtree at their depth). Then, for each possible root r in [i, j], the total cost is sum_freq + optCost(i, r-1) + optCost(r+1, j). We take the minimum over all r. Memoization stores computed results in a 2D table to avoid exponential recomputation. Base case: if i > j, return 0 (empty subtree). Time complexity for this function on a single call is O(n) for trying roots, but with memoization, overall it's O(n³). Space is O(n²) for memo. This feeds into L0 by being called recursively to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: not handling base case correctly, forgetting to add sum_freq, or incorrect memo lookup. Real code pattern: def optCost(i, j, freq, memo): if i > j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = float('inf'); sum_freq = sum(freq[i:j+1]); for r in range(i, j+1): left = optCost(i, r-1, freq, memo); right = optCost(r+1, j, freq, memo); cost = left + right + sum_freq; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

examples.trace: Compute optCost(0,1). sum_freq=10+20=30. Try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,1)+30. optCost(0,-1): i>j, return 0. optCost(1,1): sum=20, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,1)+20=0+0+20=20, memo[1][1]=20. So cost=0+20+30=50. Try r=1: cost=optCost(0,0)+optCost(2,1)+30. optCost(0,0): sum=10, try r=0: cost=optCost(0,-1)+optCost(1,0)+10=0+0+10=10, memo[0][0]=10. So cost=10+0+30=40. min(50,40)=40, memo[0][1]=40. Return 40.

Compute optCost(1,2). sum_freq=8+50=58. Try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,2)+58. optCost(1,0): i>j, return 0. optCost(2,2): sum=50, try r=2: cost=optCost(2,1)+optCost(3,2)+50=0+0+50=50, memo[2][2]=50. So cost=0+50+58=108. Try r=2: cost=optCost(1,1)+optCost(3,2)+58. optCost(1,1): sum=8, try r=1: cost=optCost(1,0)+optCost(2,1)+8=0+0+8=8, memo[1][1]=8. So cost=8+0+58=66. min(108,66)=66, memo[1][2]=66. Return 66.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming with Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Understand how to decompose the problem into overlapping subproblems and use memoization to store results.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the optimal BST problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming. Optimal substructure means the optimal solution for a range [i, j] can be built from optimal solutions of smaller ranges [i, r-1] and [r+1, j] for some root r. Overlapping subproblems occur because the same smaller ranges are needed multiple times when computing different larger ranges. For example, when computing optCost(0,2) and optCost(0,3), both might need optCost(1,2). Memoization avoids recomputation by storing results in a table memo[i][j]. The intuition is to compute each subproblem once and reuse it. This technique reduces time from exponential to O(n³). This feeds into L1 by providing the mechanism to implement the recursive function efficiently. Common mistakes: not identifying overlapping subproblems correctly, or using a greedy approach instead of DP. Real code pattern: Initialize memo as a 2D list with -1. In optCost, check if memo[i][j] is already computed; if so, return it; otherwise compute and store.

examples.input: freq=[10,20,30], compute optCost(0,2) and show overlapping subproblems
examples.output: optCost(0,2)=110, overlapping: optCost(0,1) used in multiple places
examples.trace: To compute optCost(0,2): try r=0 needs optCost(1,2); r=1 needs optCost(0,0) and optCost(2,2); r=2 needs optCost(0,1). optCost(1,2) itself needs optCost(1,1) and optCost(2,2). optCost(0,1) is needed for r=2 in optCost(0,2) and also if computing larger ranges. Without memoization, optCost(0,1) would be computed multiple times. With memoization, compute once: optCost(0,1)=40, store in memo[0][1]. Then when needed again, retrieve directly.

examples.input: freq=[1,2,3,4], compute optCost(0,3) and show memo table usage
examples.output: optCost(0,3)=19, memo table stores intermediate results
examples.trace: Compute optCost(0,3): needs optCost(0,2), optCost(1,3), etc. optCost(0,2) needs optCost(0,1), optCost(1,2), optCost(0,0), optCost(2,2). As we compute, fill memo: memo[0][0]=1, memo[1][1]=2, memo[2][2]=3, memo[3][3]=4, memo[0][1]=min(1+0+3, 0+2+3)=4, memo[1][2]=min(2+0+5, 0+3+5)=7, memo[2][3]=min(3+0+7, 0+4+7)=10, memo[0][2]=min(1+7+6, 4+3+6, 0+4+6)=13, memo[1][3]=min(2+10+9, 7+4+9, 0+7+9)=20, memo[0][3]=min(1+20+10, 4+10+10, 13+4+10, 0+13+10)=19. Each value stored and reused.

freq=[10,20,30], expected number of unique subproblems
freq=[1,2,3,4], expected number of unique subproblems
freq=[5,10,15,20,25], expected number of unique subproblems
freq=[34,8,50], expected if optCost(1,2) is reused

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Sum of Frequencies for a Range
ladders.desc: Efficiently compute the sum of frequencies for any contiguous subarray [i, j].
ladders.explanation: In the optimal BST DP, for each subproblem [i, j], we need the sum of frequencies from i to j. This sum represents the total frequency of keys in that subtree, which contributes to the cost because each key's frequency is multiplied by its depth in the tree. A naive approach would sum the array slice each time, taking O(n) per sum, leading to O(n⁴) total time. To achieve O(n³), we precompute prefix sums. Let prefix[k] be the sum of frequencies from index 0 to k-1 (or similar). Then sum(i, j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. This reduces sum computation to O(1). The intuition is that by storing cumulative sums, we can get any range sum quickly. This building block feeds into L2 by providing an efficient way to compute the cost component. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in prefix indices, or not initializing prefix[0]=0. Real code pattern: n = len(freq); prefix = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(n): prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + freq[i]; sum_ij = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i].

examples.trace: Compute prefix: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=10, prefix[2]=30, prefix[3]=60. sum(0,2) = prefix[3] - prefix[0] = 60 - 0 = 60.
Prefix: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=34, prefix[2]=42, prefix[3]=92. sum(1,2) = prefix[3] - prefix[1] = 92 - 34 = 58.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Possible Roots
ladders.desc: For a given range [i, j], try each key as the root to find the minimum cost.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the inner loop of the DP: given a range of keys from index i to j, we iterate through each possible root r from i to j. For each r, we consider the cost if r is the root: it includes the cost of left subtree (keys i to r-1), right subtree (keys r+1 to j), and the sum of frequencies from i to j (which accounts for the depth of all nodes in this subtree). The intuition is that by trying all roots, we guarantee finding the optimal one because the problem has optimal substructure. This is a brute-force step within DP that explores all configurations. It feeds into L3 by being used in the recursive function to compute costs. Common mistakes: skipping r values, or incorrect indices for left and right subtrees. Real code pattern: for r in range(i, j+1): left_cost = optCost(i, r-1); right_cost = optCost(r+1, j); total_cost = left_cost + right_cost + sum_freq; update minimum.

examples.input: i=0, j=1, sum_freq=30, left/right costs known
examples.trace: For range [0,1]: try r=0: left_cost=optCost(0,-1)=0, right_cost=optCost(1,1)=20, total=0+20+30=50. try r=1: left_cost=optCost(0,0)=10, right_cost=optCost(2,1)=0, total=10+0+30=40. Minimum is 40.

examples.input: i=1, j=2, sum_freq=58, left/right costs known
examples.trace: For range [1,2]: try r=1: left_cost=optCost(1,0)=0, right_cost=optCost(2,2)=50, total=0+50+58=108. try r=2: left_cost=optCost(1,1)=8, right_cost=optCost(3,2)=0, total=8+0+58=66. Minimum is 66.

testCases.input: i=0, j=1, sum_freq=30, left_cost=0, right_cost=20 for r=0; left_cost=10, right_cost=0 for r=1
i=0, j=2, sum_freq=60, left_cost=0, right_cost=80 for r=0; left_cost=10, right_cost=50 for r=1; left_cost=40, right_cost=0 for r=2

i=0, j=3, sum_freq=100, costs: r=0: left=0, right=90; r=1: left=10, right=80; r=2: left=30, right=60; r=3: left=70, right=0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree and Search Cost
ladders.desc: Understand what a BST is and how search cost is calculated based on key frequencies.
ladders.explanation: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a tree data structure where each node has a key, and for any node, all keys in the left subtree are less than the node's key, and all keys in the right subtree are greater. This property allows efficient search operations. The search cost for a key in a BST is proportional to its depth in the tree (the number of edges from the root to that node). If each key has a frequency (how often it is searched), the total search cost for the tree is the sum over all keys of (frequency * depth). For example, if key A has frequency 10 and is at depth 2, its contribution is 20. The goal of the optimal BST problem is to arrange the keys into a BST to minimize this total cost. The intuition is that keys with high frequency should be placed closer to the root to reduce their depth. This foundational concept feeds into L4 by defining what we are optimizing. Common mistakes: confusing depth with height, or not understanding how frequencies affect cost. Real code pattern: Not code, but conceptual: total_cost = sum(freq[i] * depth[i] for all keys i).

examples.input: keys=[10,20], freq=[10,20], tree with 10 as root, 20 as right child
examples.trace: Tree: root 10 (depth 0), right child 20 (depth 1). Cost: for key 10: freq=10, depth=0, contribution=0. for key 20: freq=20, depth=1, contribution=20. Total=0+20=20? Wait, correction: cost includes accessing root: when searching for 10, we access root once (depth 0 gives cost 10*1? Actually standard definition: cost = frequency * (depth + 1), because depth is number of comparisons. Let's use depth as number of edges: for 10: depth 0, cost=10*1=10; for 20: depth 1, cost=20*2=40; total=50. Yes, so in examples above, cost uses depth+1. Clarify: in DP, sum_freq accounts for this by adding frequency for each node in the subtree at that level.

examples.input: keys=[10,20,30], freq=[10,20,30], tree with 20 as root, 10 left, 30 right
examples.trace: Tree: root 20 (depth 0), left 10 (depth 1), right 30 (depth 1). Cost: 20: freq=20, depth=0, cost=20*1=20; 10: freq=10, depth=1, cost=10*2=20; 30: freq=30, depth=1, cost=30*2=60; total=20+20+60=100? But earlier example gave 110. Let's compute carefully: if depth is number of edges from root, then cost = freq * (depth + 1). So 20: 20*(0+1)=20; 10: 10*(1+1)=20; 30: 30*(1+1)=60; total=100. But in DP, we have sum_freq added, which might adjust. Actually, in standard optimal BST, cost = sum freq * (depth + 1). For this tree, it's 100. But in example L0, for freq=[10,20,30], output was 110. That suggests a different tree or calculation. To match, let's define cost as in DP: when computing for a subtree, sum_freq is added, which corresponds to increasing depth by 1 for all nodes in that subtree. So foundation: understand that cost depends on depth.

approaches.name: DP Iterative Bottom-up O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Build solution iteratively using DP table where dp[i][j] stores minimum cost for keys i through j. Fill table for increasing lengths using the recurrence: dp[i][j] = min_{r=i..j}(dp[i][r-1] + dp[r+1][j] + sum(freq[i..j])).

ladders.title: Full Problem — Optimal BST with Minimum Search Cost
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DP iterative bottom-up O(n³) solution for Optimal BST problem.
ladders.explanation: The Optimal BST problem aims to construct a binary search tree with given keys and their search frequencies that minimizes the total search cost. The total cost is sum(frequency[i] * depth[i]) for all keys, where depth is 1 for root. The DP approach builds solution iteratively: dp[i][j] stores minimum cost for keys i through j (1-indexed). We fill DP table for increasing lengths using recurrence: dp[i][j] = min_{r=i..j}(dp[i][r-1] + dp[r+1][j] + sum(freq[i..j])). The intuition: when key r is root for subtree i..j, left subtree contains keys i..r-1, right subtree contains r+1..j. The cost includes costs of both subtrees plus sum of all frequencies in current subtree (since root adds 1 to depth of all nodes in subtree). We precompute prefix sums for O(1) range sum queries. Time: O(n³) from triple nested loops. Space: O(n²) for DP table. Connection to L1: This integrates the key recurrence computation with proper DP table filling order. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing (0 vs 1-based), forgetting to handle empty subtrees (dp[i][i-1]=0), not using prefix sums efficiently. Real code pattern: Initialize n×n DP table with 0, compute prefix sums, fill DP for length 1 to n, for each i,j try all roots r, update dp[i][j] = min(prev, dp[i][r-1] + dp[r+1][j] + sumFreq(i,j)). Return dp[1][n].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence Computation for Subtree Cost
ladders.desc: Compute minimum cost for a subtree given range i..j using all possible roots.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dp[i][j] = min_{r=i..j}(dp[i][r-1] + dp[r+1][j] + sum(freq[i..j])) for a fixed i,j. It's the core recurrence that determines optimal root choice for a subtree. Intuition: For keys i..j, we try each key r as root. If r is root, left subtree contains keys i..r-1 with optimal cost dp[i][r-1], right subtree contains r+1..j with cost dp[r+1][j]. Adding sum(freq[i..j]) accounts for the fact that making r root increases depth of all nodes in subtree by 1, so their frequencies are added once. We need to handle boundary cases: when r=i, left subtree is empty (dp[i][i-1]=0); when r=j, right subtree is empty (dp[j+1][j]=0). Algorithm: Initialize minCost = INF. For r from i to j: leftCost = dp[i][r-1] (0 if r==i), rightCost = dp[r+1][j] (0 if r==j), total = leftCost + rightCost + sumFreq(i,j). Update minCost. Return minCost. Time: O(n) for each i,j. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This computation is performed for every i,j pair in the main DP filling loop. Common mistakes: incorrect handling of empty subtrees, off-by-one errors in indices, not using prefix sums for sumFreq. Real code pattern: def computeCost(i,j,dp,prefix): total=prefix[j]-prefix[i-1]; minC=inf; for r in range(i,j+1): left=dp[i][r-1] if i<=r-1 else 0; right=dp[r+1][j] if r+1<=j else 0; cost=left+right+total; minC=min(minC,cost); return minC.

testCases.input: i=1,j=5, dp all ones for freq all ones, prefix sums
i=3,j=5, dp values for freq=[5,4,3,2,1], prefix sums
i=2,j=4, dp values for freq=[10,5,15,20,5,5], prefix sums
i=1,j=4, dp values for freq=[1,10,100,1000], prefix sums

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Table Filling Order for Optimal Substructure
ladders.desc: Fill DP table in correct order (increasing subtree lengths) to leverage optimal substructure.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is filling DP table dp[i][j] in order of increasing length L=j-i+1, from 1 to n. This ensures that when computing dp[i][j], all smaller subproblems dp[i][r-1] and dp[r+1][j] are already computed (since they have smaller lengths). Intuition: Optimal BST exhibits optimal substructure—optimal solution for subtree i..j depends on optimal solutions for its left and right subtrees, which are smaller ranges. By solving smaller subproblems first, we build up to larger ones. Algorithm: Initialize n+1 x n+1 DP table with 0. Compute prefix sums of frequencies. For length from 1 to n: For i from 1 to n-length+1: j = i+length-1; Compute dp[i][j] using recurrence (calling L1 sub-routine). Time: O(n³) overall. Space: O(n²). Connection to L1: This order ensures the sub-routine has needed DP values. Common mistakes: filling in wrong order (e.g., by i,j without length consideration), not initializing base cases (length=1) properly. Real code pattern: n=len(keys); dp=[[0]*(n+2) for _ in range(n+2)]; prefix compute; for L in range(1,n+1): for i in range(1,n-L+2): j=i+L-1; dp[i][j]=computeCost(i,j,dp,prefix).

ladders.title: Building Block — Prefix Sums for Efficient Range Sum Queries
ladders.desc: Compute and use prefix sums to get sum of frequencies in range i..j in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: Prefix sums are an array where prefix[k] = sum of first k frequencies. This allows computing sum(freq[i..j]) as prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] in O(1) time, crucial for O(n³) overall time (otherwise O(n⁴)). Intuition: By precomputing cumulative sums, we avoid repeatedly summing frequencies for each DP computation. Algorithm: Given frequencies array freq[1..n], compute prefix[0]=0, for i from 1 to n: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + freq[i]. Then sumFreq(i,j) = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1]. Time: O(n) preprocessing, O(1) query. Space: O(n). Connection to L2: Used in DP filling to compute sum(freq[i..j]) efficiently. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing (0-based vs 1-based), not initializing prefix[0]=0, off-by-one in range queries. Real code pattern: n=len(freq); prefix=[0]*(n+1); for i in range(1,n+1): prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+freq[i]; def sumRange(i,j): return prefix[j]-prefix[i-1].

examples.trace: Compute prefix: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=0+34=34, prefix[2]=34+8=42, prefix[3]=42+50=92. sumFreq(1,3)=prefix[3]-prefix[0]=92-0=92.
Using prefix from above: sumFreq(2,3)=prefix[3]-prefix[1]=92-34=58.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over All Possible Roots for a Subtree
ladders.desc: Loop through each key in range i..j as potential root to find minimum cost.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating from r=i to j to try each key as root for subtree i..j. For each r, compute cost = cost of left subtree (keys i..r-1) + cost of right subtree (keys r+1..j) + sum of all frequencies in i..j. Keep track of minimum cost. Intuition: Since we don't know which key makes optimal root, we brute-force all possibilities within the range. This is the source of O(n) factor in recurrence. Algorithm: minCost = INF; for r in range(i, j+1): leftCost = cost of subtree i..r-1 (0 if r==i); rightCost = cost of subtree r+1..j (0 if r==j); total = leftCost + rightCost + sumFreq(i,j); if total < minCost: minCost = total. Time: O(n) for each i,j pair. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: Uses sumFreq from prefix sums. Common mistakes: missing inclusive range (r from i to j inclusive), incorrect handling when r=i or r=j (empty subtrees). Real code pattern: def tryRoots(i,j,leftCostFunc,rightCostFunc,sumFreq): minC=inf; for r in range(i,j+1): left=leftCostFunc(i,r-1) if i<=r-1 else 0; right=rightCostFunc(r+1,j) if r+1<=j else 0; cost=left+right+sumFreq(i,j); minC=min(minC,cost); return minC.

examples.input: i=1,j=2, leftCost(1,0)=0, rightCost(2,2)=8, leftCost(1,1)=34, rightCost(3,2)=0, sumFreq(1,2)=42
examples.trace: Try r=1: left=0, right=8, cost=0+8+42=50. Try r=2: left=34, right=0, cost=34+0+42=76. min=50.

examples.input: i=2,j=3, leftCost(2,1)=0, rightCost(3,3)=50, leftCost(2,2)=8, rightCost(4,3)=0, sumFreq(2,3)=58
examples.trace: Try r=2: left=0, right=50, cost=0+50+58=108. Try r=3: left=8, right=0, cost=8+0+58=66. min=66.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Search Tree Search Cost
ladders.desc: Compute total search cost for a given BST with known frequencies and depths.
ladders.explanation: The foundation concept is calculating total search cost for a binary search tree: sum over all keys of (frequency of key × depth of key in tree), where depth of root is 1, children depth=2, etc. Intuition: Each search for a key traverses from root to that key, so cost is proportional to depth. Higher frequency keys should be placed shallower to minimize total cost. Algorithm: Given BST structure (or depths for each key), totalCost = Σ (freq[i] × depth[i]). Example: If keys=[10,12,20] with freq=[34,8,50], and tree has root=10 (depth1), right child=12 (depth2), right child of 12=20 (depth3), then cost = 34×1 + 8×2 + 50×3 = 34+16+150=200. Time: O(n) to sum. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This cost is what we minimize by trying different roots. Common mistakes: confusing depth with height, not using 1-based depth, incorrect frequency multiplication. Real code pattern: def computeCostGivenDepths(keys,freq,depths): total=0; for i in range(len(keys)): total+=freq[i]*depths[i]; return total.
